headline: Africa's Clean Cooking Revolution Moves From Promise to Action
meta_description: African nations secure $900M in new funding to bring clean cooking to millions, with $740M already deployed across 22 countries.
summary_short: Nearly a billion Africans still cook over charcoal and firewood. Now, real money is flowing to change that.
body:
The numbers tell two different stories. Nearly one billion people across Africa cook over charcoal or firewood daily, inhaling fumes that kill an estimated 850,000 people every year. Women and children bear the heaviest burden, spending the most hours near smoky fires.

But there's another story unfolding now—one of momentum shifting from talk to action. Last week, African countries secured $900 million in fresh financial commitments for clean cooking technologies like ethanol stoves, biogas systems, and electric cookers. Combined with $2.2 billion pledged at Africa's inaugural Clean Cooking Summit in Paris in 2024, the total now exceeds $3.1 billion.

What matters most: $740 million of those earlier pledges has already been deployed across 22 countries. The money isn't sitting on paper. It's working.

Kenyan President William Ruto put it plainly during the virtual meeting convened by the International Energy Agency and Kenya: "Ambition alone is not enough. It must be backed by investment."

Those clean cooking technologies address the problem at its source—in the kitchen itself, not downstream. The next summit is expected later this year. Reaching everyone will cost far more than $3.1 billion. But for the first time, the pace of progress is accelerating.
